text.title: Guardrails Basics Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for a layered defense-in-depth guardrail stack.

rule.statement: Guardrail stack has ≥4 distinct layers (regex pre-filter, length cap, output schema, LLM classifier). Single-layer setups fail to adversarial inputs.
rule.rationale: Each layer catches a different failure mode; one suffices for only its narrow surface.

rule.statement: Layer order is cheap → expensive. Regex (microseconds) before LLM classifier (300-800 ms). Above-order layers reject ~80% of bad inputs without paying classifier cost.
rule.rationale: Wrong order multiplies cost without changing coverage.

rule.statement: Per-layer block rates emit to telemetry. One layer at 100% block share is a signal that other layers are mis-tuned.
LLM classifier is always last. It is non-deterministic, slow, and a single point of failure if first.
rule.rationale: Putting the classifier first costs latency for inputs that other layers would have rejected and creates an over-reliance.

rule.statement: Default is fail-closed: on classifier exception, on regex unavailable, on schema error → block the call. Fail-open is opt-in with sign-off.
rule.rationale: Fail-open under panic mode hides incident severity and may release adversarial outputs.

rule.statement: Preconditions hold → wire the 4-layer spec per procedure.
